| Collections cost | 6% of collected | 6% of collected |

**Q:** *План умножил 46,000 × $308. По какой строке denial?*

📖 План применил **базовые 8%** к кодам, у которых **31%** — prior-auth-тяжёлые specialty-коды деклайнятся вчетверо чаще (Stern: collections — центральная механика провайдера).
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
**EXHIBIT A — reimbursement profile**

| Параметр | База-меню | **Новые specialty-коды** |
|---|---|---|
| Denial rate | 8% | **31%** *(prior authorization, новизна кодов)* |
| Collections cost | 6% of collected | 6% of collected |

**Q:** *План умножил 46,000 × $308. По какой строке denial?*

📖 План применил **базовые 8%** к кодам, у которых **31%** — prior-auth-тяжёлые specialty-коды деклайнятся вчетверо чаще (Stern: collections — центральная механика провайдера).

steps.label: ГЛАВНЫЙ NUMERIC + [9b]  (5 min)
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
def profit(denial):                       # одна функция [9b]
    return 46_000*308*(1-denial)*0.94 - 10_900_000

naive   = profit(0.08)   # = +$1,352,486.40  -> GO
correct = profit(0.31)   # = -$1,710,635.20  -> NO GO
```

### ⛔ **ФЛИП: +$1,352,486 → −$1,710,635**

**Sensitivity ([9b], та же функция):** breakeven denial = **18.16%**. `profit(0.20) = −$243К` — даже при вдвое лучшей работе с prior-auth, чем сегодняшние 31%, меню на грани.

steps.label: Explain  (2 min)
steps.candidate_md: Gross reimbursement — это **счёт, а не деньги**: лаборатория живёт на collected. Новые коды падают на prior-auth: страховщик не знает код → deny by default → appeal-цикл. 31% — не навсегда (зрелые меню приходят к 10–12%), но бизнес-кейс, посчитанный по 8% с первого дня, финансирует программу деньгами, которых **три года не будет**.
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
Gross reimbursement — это **счёт, а не деньги**: лаборатория живёт на collected. Новые коды падают на prior-auth: страховщик не знает код → deny by default → appeal-цикл. 31% — не навсегда (зрелые меню приходят к 10–12%), но бизнес-кейс, посчитанный по 8% с первого дня, финансирует программу деньгами, которых **три года не будет**.

steps.label: Синтез  (4 min)
steps.candidate_md: > **We recommend Diagnexa does not launch the menu as modelled — at the actual 31% denial rate of new specialty codes, it loses $1.7 million a year, not earns $3.3 million.**
steps.interviewer_md:
>
> Two facts. First, the plan multiplied volume by gross reimbursement and applied the base menu's 8% denial rate; new prior-auth-heavy codes deny at **31%**, and net of collections cost the revenue is $9.2 million against $10.9 million of cost. Second, the breakeven denial is 18.2% — reachable for a mature menu, but not on day one: new codes deny by default until payer policies catch up, typically over two to three years.
>
> **Gross reimbursement is an invoice. A laboratory eats what it collects.**
>
> Two risks. Internally, the same 8% blended denial assumption is likely inflating every menu-expansion case in the pipeline. Externally, denial rates on these codes are payer-policy-driven — one national payer adding coverage moves the answer more than anything we control.
>
> Next steps. Within 45 days, launch a prior-auth desk pilot on the five highest-volume codes — mature-menu comparators run 10–12%, below the 18.2% breakeven, so the menu becomes viable exactly when the collections capability exists. Sequence the analyzer lease to that milestone, not to the calendar.

cases.title: Snack to the Future (Snackora Foods)
cases.header_md:
## Case 71 — Snack to the Future (Snackora Foods) · M&A · Medium · Trap-overstate

> **ПЛАШКА** 
> **Тип:** M&A · **Индустрия:** CPG snacks · **Формат:** F1  
> **Difficulty:** 🟡 Medium · **Depth:** 🔥 STRONG · **Соответствие:** ↑выше  
> **T/C:** Trap · **Naive:** #14 stranded cost · **Время:** —  
> **Экспонаты (1):** table — Exhibit A — Divisional P&L (shared distribut  
> **Reveal:** on_request
> **optimal_entry:** 4  
>  
> **Слои insight:** ① F1 скрытая корректировка ② **stranded OH: $330M — частный случай, где 100% оверхеда избегаем; реально 35% → $70M** + breakeven 17.5%

**M&A · CPG snacks · Medium · Trap · naive #14 stranded cost · gen C**
*Registry: Snack to the Future*

**Grounding (Stern CPG p.14):** distribution & inventory management is a shared *cost line*; portfolio cannibalization; sales-to-retailers lower margin than DTC. → The stranded-cost trap: shared distribution overhead does not leave when a brand is divested.
cases.prompt_md: Snackora Foods (three divisions: Premium snacks, Core biscuits, Value crackers) is considering **selling its Value-crackers division**, which reports a loss. A buyer offers **$150M**. The board's deck claims the sale creates **$330M** of value. Should Snackora sell, and is the board's number right? (WACC 10%, perpetuity.)

exhibits.title: Exhibit A — Divisional P&L (shared distribution OH allocated evenly $40M each).
exhibits.body_md:
**Exhibit A — Divisional P&L (shared distribution OH allocated evenly $40M each).**

| Division | Revenue | Direct cost | Alloc. distribution OH | Reported EBIT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Premium snacks | 300 | 204 | 40 | **+56** |
| Core biscuits | 260 | 182 | 40 | **+38** |
| Value crackers | 180 | 158 | 40 | **−18** |
| **Group** | | | 120 | **+76** |

steps.label: Framework.
steps.candidate_md: (candidate structures divest decision: standalone value of division vs offer + stranded-cost check.)
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
(candidate structures divest decision: standalone value of division vs offer + stranded-cost check.)

steps.label: Mental math (30–60s):
steps.candidate_md: group EBIT 76 → base value 76/0.10 = **$760M**. Prime the anchor before touching the offer.
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
group EBIT 76 → base value 76/0.10 = **$760M**. Prime the anchor before touching the offer.

steps.label: Naive path (the board's $330M).
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
WACC = Decimal('0.10')
# Naive: closing removes the full −18 loss AND the full $40M allocated OH is "saved"
naive_group_ebit = 76 + 18           # loss vanishes -> 94
naive_value  = 94/0.10 + 150         # = 1090
base_value   = 76/0.10               # = 760
naive_value_created = 1090 - 760     # = 330  ✓ matches board deck
```
Board says **+$330M**.

steps.label: The hidden adjustment (F1).
steps.candidate_md: Of the $40M allocated to Value crackers, only the **dedicated packaging line (35% = $14M)** leaves on divestiture. The **shared distribution network + inventory system ($26M)** is *stranded* — it re-allocates to the surviving divisions (Stern CPG: distribution is a shared cost line).
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
avoidable_oh = 40 * 0.35          # 14 leaves
stranded_oh  = 40 * 0.65          # 26 stays
true_contribution = 180 - 158 - 14   # = +8  (division is actually POSITIVE)
```

steps.label: Correct value created.
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
group_ebit_after_sale = 76 - 8    # lose the +8 contribution -> 68
correct_value = 68/0.10 + 150     # = 830
correct_value_created = 830 - 760 # = +70
```
**Correct: sell creates +$70M, not +$330M.** Same *direction* (sell is accretive), magnitude overstated **371%**.

steps.label: Sensitivity (one function, base + stress).
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
def value_created(avoidable_share, offer=150):
    avoid = 40*avoidable_share
    contrib = 180 - 158 - avoid
    return ((76-contrib)/0.10 + offer) - 76/0.10
# 35% -> 70 | 55% -> 150 | 75% -> 230 | 100% -> 330 (== naive: the special case!)
# breakeven avoidable share = 7/40 = 0.175 -> below 17.5%, sale destroys value
```
**Teaching point:** the board's $330M is the *special case where 100% of allocated overhead is assumed avoidable.* Reality (35%) gives $70M.

steps.label: Brainstorm
steps.candidate_md: (min 6, MECE — is the OH really stranded?): shared truck routes, shared DC leases, shared ERP/inventory system, shared trade-marketing team, shared procurement contracts, shared regional sales force. (Revenue-side vs cost-side split.)
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
(min 6, MECE — is the OH really stranded?): shared truck routes, shared DC leases, shared ERP/inventory system, shared trade-marketing team, shared procurement contracts, shared regional sales force. (Revenue-side vs cost-side split.)

steps.label: Recommendation (Pyramid).
steps.candidate_md:
*"Snackora should sell Value crackers — but the board's $330M thesis is fiction; real value created is +$70M."* Facts: division truly contributes +$8M (not −$18M) once stranded OH is stripped; only $14M of $40M OH is avoidable; $26M re-allocates to survivors. Risks: (internal) survivors' reported margins drop as stranded OH lands on them; (external) buyer may renegotiate below $150M once diligence reveals the thin standalone economics. Next steps: 90-day avoidable-cost audit of the distribution contract before signing; renegotiate transition-services agreement so stranded OH is temporary.

**Six-check table (#71).**

| Check | Result | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|
| [1] Rounding chain | round once at end | 0.00 (all integer inputs) |
| [3] Overstatement base | vs final +70 (not intermediate) | 371.43% correct base |
| [5] Consistency shown | $8 contribution derives from shown $14 avoidable | 0.00 |
| [6] Metric unity | perpetuity /0.10 both paths | one formula ✓ |
| [9b] Sensitivity one fn | same `value_created()` | 0.00 |
| 100%-avoidable = naive | 330 == 330 | exact tautology ✓ |
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
*"Snackora should sell Value crackers — but the board's $330M thesis is fiction; real value created is +$70M."* Facts: division truly contributes +$8M (not −$18M) once stranded OH is stripped; only $14M of $40M OH is avoidable; $26M re-allocates to survivors. Risks: (internal) survivors' reported margins drop as stranded OH lands on them; (external) buyer may renegotiate below $150M once diligence reveals the thin standalone economics. Next steps: 90-day avoidable-cost audit of the distribution contract before signing; renegotiate transition-services agreement so stranded OH is temporary.

**Six-check table (#71).**

| Check | Result | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|
| [1] Rounding chain | round once at end | 0.00 (all integer inputs) |
| [3] Overstatement base | vs final +70 (not intermediate) | 371.43% correct base |
| [5] Consistency shown | $8 contribution derives from shown $14 avoidable | 0.00 |
| [6] Metric unity | perpetuity /0.10 both paths | one formula ✓ |
| [9b] Sensitivity one fn | same `value_created()` | 0.00 |
| 100%-avoidable = naive | 330 == 330 | exact tautology ✓ |

cases.title: Silicon Valley of the Dolls (Maskwright Systems)
cases.header_md:
## Case 72 — Silicon Valley of the Dolls (Maskwright Systems) · M&A · **Hard** · Trap-flip

> **ПЛАШКА** 
> **Тип:** M&A · **Индустрия:** Semiconductor · **Формат:** F6  
> **Difficulty:** 🔴 Hard · **Depth:** 🔥 STRONG · **Соответствие:** OK  
> **T/C:** Trap · **Naive:** #1 hist-vs-replacement · **Время:** —  
> **Экспонаты (2):** table — Exhibit A — Target tool-line cost basis.; table — Exhibit B — the buyer-specific twist (F6, mi  
> **Reveal:** auto, on_request
> **optimal_entry:** 4  
>  
> **Слои insight:** ① naive historical cost ② mental math sign-check ③ hypothesis-update перед Exhibit B ④ replacement cost + **metric unity**

**M&A · Semiconductor · Hard · Trap · naive #1 hist-vs-replacement · gen C**
*Registry: Silicon Valley of the Dolls*

**Grounding (Stern Technology p.15):** hardware = component COGS; product obsolescence; reduced product life spans. Fab-tool components reprice fast → historical build cost badly understates replacement cost.
cases.prompt_md: Maskwright Systems (lithography-tool maker) is bidding for a smaller tool maker. The seller asks **$587.5M**. Maskwright's finance team values the target at ~$1.67B on the target's books and calls it a bargain. Should Maskwright buy at $587.5M? (WACC 12%, perpetuity.)

exhibits.title: Exhibit A — Target tool-line cost basis.
exhibits.body_md:
**Exhibit A — Target tool-line cost basis.**

| Tool line | Historical build cost ($M) | Current replacement index |
|---|---|---|
| DUV source | 80 | 1.25 |
| Optics column | 120 | 1.55 |
| Wafer stage | 60 | 1.35 |
| Vacuum system | 40 | 1.20 |
| Control / EUV | 100 | 1.70 |
| **Total / weighted** | **400** | **1.4625** |

Target ships **40 tools/yr** at **$12M/tool**; historical variable cost **$7M/tool**.

exhibits.title: Exhibit B — the buyer-specific twist (F6, missing info the candidate must request).
exhibits.body_md:
**Exhibit B — the buyer-specific twist (F6, missing info the candidate must request).** Maskwright's acquisition triggers a **2-year export-control review** on the Control/EUV linchpin line; **35% of output is un-shippable** during review.
```python
naive   = dcf_value(7,       0)   # 1666.67  BUY
correct = dcf_value(repl_vc, 2)   #  545.80  WALK  (ask 587.5 -> overpay 41.70 = 7.1%)
# overstatement (naive vs correct) = 205.36%
```
**Flip: naive BUY → correct WALK.** Driven by the export twist ($42M / 7.1% margin), a Maskwright-specific driver (passes interchangeability — the *seller's* standalone is fine).

steps.label: Framework.
steps.candidate_md: (standalone value vs ask; what cost basis is correct; what could break the perpetuity.)
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
(standalone value vs ask; what cost basis is correct; what could break the perpetuity.)

steps.label: Naive (historical cost).
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
WACC = Decimal('0.12')
naive_ebitda = 40*(12-7)        # = 200
naive_value  = 200/0.12         # = 1666.67  -> vs ask 587.5 -> BUY (looks cheap)
```

steps.label: Mental math sign-check (30–60s):
steps.candidate_md: replacement index ≈1.46, so real variable cost ≈ 7×1.46 ≈ 10.2, margin per tool collapses from $5M to ≈$1.8M. "Margin down ~65% → value can't be $1.67B." Flag before the model.
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
replacement index ≈1.46, so real variable cost ≈ 7×1.46 ≈ 10.2, margin per tool collapses from $5M to ≈$1.8M. "Margin down ~65% → value can't be $1.67B." Flag before the model.

steps.label: Hypothesis-update (before Exhibit B):
steps.candidate_md: *what should the replacement-cost value be, and what else could a buyer-specific issue do?* Strong hypothesis: value falls but stays > ask. (Exhibit B partly confirms — value does fall to ~$588M — and partly breaks it: a buyer-specific export review pushes it *below* ask.)
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
*what should the replacement-cost value be, and what else could a buyer-specific issue do?* Strong hypothesis: value falls but stays > ask. (Exhibit B partly confirms — value does fall to ~$588M — and partly breaks it: a buyer-specific export review pushes it *below* ask.)

steps.label: Correct: replacement cost + metric unity.
steps.candidate_md: Both branches use the **identical** perpetuity-DCF function; only the cost base and restriction differ.
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
def dcf_value(varcost_per_tool, restricted_years=0):   # ONE formula, both branches
    ebitda_full = 40*(12 - varcost_per_tool)
    if restricted_years == 0:
        return ebitda_full/0.12
    ebitda_restr = 40*(1-0.35)*(12 - varcost_per_tool)  # export review: 35% un-shippable
    pv = sum(ebitda_restr/(1.12**y) for y in range(1, restricted_years+1))  # yrs 1..R
    return pv + (ebitda_full/0.12)/(1.12**restricted_years)

repl_vc = 7 * 1.4625                       # = 10.2375
standalone = dcf_value(repl_vc, 0)         # = 587.50  <-- seller's fair ask (DERIVED)
ask = standalone                           # kept EXACT (not rounded to 588)
```
**The ask is derived from the target's fair standalone**, not reverse-fit.

steps.label: Sensitivity (export review length, one function).
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
# 0yr = 587.50 (== ask, exact construction breakeven) | 1yr = 565.47 | 2yr = 545.80 | 3yr = 528.23
```
**Because the ask equals the unrestricted standalone exactly, *any* export delay tips it to WALK.** M&A lesson: paying full standalone means any buyer-introduced friction destroys value.

steps.label: Brainstorm
steps.candidate_md: (what else could a buyer trigger that a seller-standalone view misses): antitrust divestiture, key-engineer retention flight, customer-overlap concentration, IP-license change-of-control clauses, capex to re-tool controlled line, financing cost of the premium. (Buyer-side vs deal-structure split.)
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
(what else could a buyer trigger that a seller-standalone view misses): antitrust divestiture, key-engineer retention flight, customer-overlap concentration, IP-license change-of-control clauses, capex to re-tool controlled line, financing cost of the premium. (Buyer-side vs deal-structure split.)

steps.label: Recommendation (Pyramid).
steps.candidate_md:
*"Maskwright should walk away at $587.5M."* Facts: replacement-cost economics cut standalone to $587.5M (matching ask); the buyer-triggered 2-year export review destroys a further $42M, putting fair value at $545.8M; the $1.67B book value is a historical-cost illusion (component COGS repriced ~46%). Risks: (internal) walking may lose the linchpin EUV capability to a rival bidder; (external/regulatory) export-control scope could widen beyond 2 years. Next steps: request a binding export-control opinion within 30 days; re-approach at ≤$546M or with a review-contingent earn-out.

**Six-check table (#72).**

| Check | Result | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|
| [1] Rounding chain | exact intermediates (repl_vc 10.2375, standalone 587.50) | 0.00 |
| [5] Consistency shown | ask = shown standalone, kept exact (not 588) | rounding to 588 would inject +0.50 false gap → avoided |
| [6] Metric unity | both branches call `dcf_value` (perpetuity DCF @12%) | one formula ✓ verbatim |
| [9] Sensitivity full recompute | multi-year chain recomputed, not delta | 0.00 |
| [9b] Sensitivity one fn | same `dcf_value`, args only | 0.00 |
| Breakeven-at-0 tautology | dcf_value(repl_vc,0) == ask, exact | 0.00 (construction, not fitted) |
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
*"Maskwright should walk away at $587.5M."* Facts: replacement-cost economics cut standalone to $587.5M (matching ask); the buyer-triggered 2-year export review destroys a further $42M, putting fair value at $545.8M; the $1.67B book value is a historical-cost illusion (component COGS repriced ~46%). Risks: (internal) walking may lose the linchpin EUV capability to a rival bidder; (external/regulatory) export-control scope could widen beyond 2 years. Next steps: request a binding export-control opinion within 30 days; re-approach at ≤$546M or with a review-contingent earn-out.

**Six-check table (#72).**

| Check | Result | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|
| [1] Rounding chain | exact intermediates (repl_vc 10.2375, standalone 587.50) | 0.00 |
| [5] Consistency shown | ask = shown standalone, kept exact (not 588) | rounding to 588 would inject +0.50 false gap → avoided |
| [6] Metric unity | both branches call `dcf_value` (perpetuity DCF @12%) | one formula ✓ verbatim |
| [9] Sensitivity full recompute | multi-year chain recomputed, not delta | 0.00 |
| [9b] Sensitivity one fn | same `dcf_value`, args only | 0.00 |
| Breakeven-at-0 tautology | dcf_value(repl_vc,0) == ask, exact | 0.00 (construction, not fitted) |

cases.title: The Sound of Medicine (Wellspire Health)
cases.header_md:
## Case 73 — The Sound of Medicine (Wellspire Health) · M&A sell-side · Medium · **Clean**

> **ПЛАШКА** 
> **Тип:** M&A (sell-side) · **Индустрия:** Hospitals · **Формат:** —  
> **Difficulty:** 🟡 Medium · **Depth:** 🔥 STRONG · **Соответствие:** ↑выше  
> **T/C:** Clean · **Naive:** — · **Время:** —  
> **Экспонаты (1):** table — Exhibit A — Hospital P&L + occupancy.  
> **Reveal:** on_request
> **optimal_entry:** 2  
>  
> **Слои insight:** ① вывести экономику из occupancy ② отвергнуть H1 payer mix ③ H2 рычаг структурный ④ H3: **ловушка #71 здесь НЕ работает** → **CLEAN**

**M&A (sell-side) · Hospitals · Medium · Clean · gen C**
*Registry: The Sound of Medicine*

**Grounding (Stern Healthcare Providers p.16):** revenue = collections from third-party payers + government reimbursement; key metric = **bed capacity utilization**; costs = high fixed facilities + physicians. → Low occupancy drives dilution through fixed-cost drag.

**Clean-case purpose.** A candidate trained on stranded-cost traps (#71) will *hunt* for a reversal here and must **not invent one**. The overhead is genuinely dedicated (separate metros) → the sale is clean. The skill is validating that no hidden error exists and saying so.
cases.prompt_md: Wellspire Health runs four hospitals in separate metros. **Riverside** (the weakest) has a buyer offering **$220M**. The board debates three theories for Riverside's low margin. Should Wellspire sell? (WACC 10%.)

exhibits.title: Exhibit A — Hospital P&L + occupancy.
exhibits.body_md:
**Exhibit A — Hospital P&L + occupancy.**

| Hospital | Revenue | Direct cost | Dedicated OH | EBIT | Beds | Occupancy |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Central | 420 | 310 | 60 | +50 | 400 | 0.82 |
| Harbor | 360 | 266 | 55 | +39 | 340 | 0.78 |
| Summit | 300 | 222 | 48 | +30 | 300 | 0.80 |
| Riverside | *derived* | *derived* | 44 | **−16.21** | 280 | **0.61** |

steps.label: Framework + open hypotheses.
steps.candidate_md: Board's three theories: H1 payer mix, H2 utilization, H3 stranded-cost illusion (the #71 trap).
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
Board's three theories: H1 payer mix, H2 utilization, H3 stranded-cost illusion (the #71 trap).

steps.label: Derive Riverside economics from occupancy (not asserted).
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
# revenue scales with occupancy off peer benchmark ($1.05M/bed at 80% occ)
riv_rev = 1.05*280*(0.61/0.80)                 # = 224.18
# cost split: 60% fixed (facilities/physicians), 40% variable (nurses/supplies flex)
full_dc = 0.775*280                            # 217 at capacity staffing
riv_dc  = full_dc*0.60 + full_dc*0.40*(0.61/0.80)   # = 130.20 + 66.19 = 196.39
riv_ebit = 224.18 - 196.39 - 44                 # = −16.21   (derived, not fitted)
```

steps.label: H1 (payer mix): reject.
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
# gov-payer 58% @ 80% of commercial rate; peers 40%. Even at peer mix:
rev_peer_mix = (224.18/(0.58*0.80+0.42))*(0.40*0.80+0.60)   # = 233.30
ebit_peer_mix = 233.30 - 196.39 - 44                        # = −7.08  (STILL dilutive)
```
Payer mix explains part, but Riverside stays negative even fixed → not a reason to keep.

steps.label: H2 (utilization): lever exists but structurally unfixable.
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
ebit_at_80pct = 1.05*280 - (130 + 0.775*280*0.40) - 44   # = +33  (would be worth keeping)
```
**The honest nuance:** *if* occupancy could reach peer 80%, Riverside earns +$33M. But the low fill is **structural metro bed-oversupply**, not Wellspire-fixable → H2 rejected as *actionable*, not as arithmetic. (This is what makes it a Medium clean, not trivial — candidate must see the lever exists but isn't theirs to pull.)

steps.label: H3 (stranded illusion): the #71 trap does NOT apply.
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
# separate metros -> overhead is DEDICATED (staff + facility), 100% avoidable on sale.
# Counterfactual: sale would flip to KEEP only if avoidable share < (224-196)/44 = 0.6316
# i.e. if >37% were stranded. It is 0% stranded here -> robust SELL, no illusion.
```

steps.label: Brainstorm
steps.candidate_md: (how to confirm no hidden error before signing): verify OH is truly dedicated (no shared services), confirm occupancy is structural not seasonal, check the buyer isn't a payer-mix arbitrageur, test whether a physician group would lease vs buy, stress the reimbursement environment, confirm no cross-metro referral loss. (Confirm-clean checklist.)
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
(how to confirm no hidden error before signing): verify OH is truly dedicated (no shared services), confirm occupancy is structural not seasonal, check the buyer isn't a payer-mix arbitrageur, test whether a physician group would lease vs buy, stress the reimbursement environment, confirm no cross-metro referral loss. (Confirm-clean checklist.)

steps.label: Recommendation (Pyramid).
steps.candidate_md:
*"Wellspire should sell Riverside for $220M — and there is no stranded-cost trap here to reverse that."* Facts: Riverside is genuinely dilutive at −$16M driven by structural 61% occupancy; overhead is 100% dedicated so it leaves cleanly; even correcting payer mix leaves it negative. Risks: (internal) loss of cross-metro referral volume; (external/regulatory) buyer's certificate-of-need transfer could stall closing. Next steps: 30-day dedication audit of Riverside overhead; confirm no shared-services contamination before signing.

**Six-check table (#73).**

| Check | Result | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|
| Derive-first | Riverside −16.21 derived from occupancy + 60/40 split, not asserted | exact −16.21 shown (not rounded); final $382.10 traces from it |
| [1] Rounding chain | round once at end, exact intermediates shown | 0.00 |
| [5] Consistency of shown | shown −16.21/224.18/196.39 = exact; final 382.10 derives from them | 0.00 |
| H1/H2/H3 all on derived numbers | −7.08 / +33.00 / 0.6316 breakeven | consistent |
| [6] Metric unity | perpetuity /0.10 keep & sell | one formula ✓ |
| Clean = naive==correct | 382.10 == 382.10 | 0.00% difference ✓ |
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
*"Wellspire should sell Riverside for $220M — and there is no stranded-cost trap here to reverse that."* Facts: Riverside is genuinely dilutive at −$16M driven by structural 61% occupancy; overhead is 100% dedicated so it leaves cleanly; even correcting payer mix leaves it negative. Risks: (internal) loss of cross-metro referral volume; (external/regulatory) buyer's certificate-of-need transfer could stall closing. Next steps: 30-day dedication audit of Riverside overhead; confirm no shared-services contamination before signing.

**Six-check table (#73).**

| Check | Result | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|
| Derive-first | Riverside −16.21 derived from occupancy + 60/40 split, not asserted | exact −16.21 shown (not rounded); final $382.10 traces from it |
| [1] Rounding chain | round once at end, exact intermediates shown | 0.00 |
| [5] Consistency of shown | shown −16.21/224.18/196.39 = exact; final 382.10 derives from them | 0.00 |
| H1/H2/H3 all on derived numbers | −7.08 / +33.00 / 0.6316 breakeven | consistent |
| [6] Metric unity | perpetuity /0.10 keep & sell | one formula ✓ |
| Clean = naive==correct | 382.10 == 382.10 | 0.00% difference ✓ |

cases.title: Some Like It Hot (Others Cold) (Fizzano Beverages)
cases.header_md:
## Case 74 — Some Like It Hot (Others Cold) (Fizzano Beverages) · M&A bolt-on · **Easy** · Trap-flip

> **ПЛАШКА** 
> **Тип:** M&A (bolt-on) · **Индустрия:** Beverages · **Формат:** F1  
> **Difficulty:** 🟢 Easy · **Depth:** ⚠️ STANDARD · **Соответствие:** ↑выше  
> **T/C:** Trap · **Naive:** #4 gross-vs-net · **Время:** —  
> **Экспонаты (1):** table — встроенная таблица в шаге  
> **Reveal:** inline
> **optimal_entry:** 4  
>  
> **Слои insight:** ① gross synergy ② F1 скрытые dis-synergies → net

**M&A (bolt-on) · Beverages · Easy · Trap · naive #4 gross-vs-net · gen C**
*Registry: Some Like It Hot (Others Cold)*

**Grounding (Stern CPG p.14 + Kellogg Zephyr beverage economics):** shelf placement vs competition; portfolio cannibalization; distribution as a shared cost line; concentrated, promo-driven, price-sensitive market where brand premium must stay in line. **[Grounding deviation flagged: no Kellogg 2020 Beverages spread in project files — used Kellogg-2012 Zephyr case economics + Stern CPG. Named for reviewer.]**
cases.prompt_md: Fizzano Beverages (cold sodas) is bolting on a **hot-beverage brand**. Management's deck claims **$30M/yr** in distribution synergy (shared trucks/routes) and asks Fizzano to pay a **$120M premium** over standalone. Does the deal work? (WACC 10%, perpetuity.)

steps.label: Framework.
steps.candidate_md: (gross synergy vs dis-synergy; is the premium justified by *net*?)
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
(gross synergy vs dis-synergy; is the premium justified by *net*?)

steps.label: Naive (gross synergy).
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
gross_value = 30/0.10       # = 300  -> vs premium 120 -> DEAL WORKS (looks great)
```

steps.label: Mental math (30–60s):
steps.candidate_md: "$30M/yr ÷ 10% = $300M, premium only $120M → obviously yes." Deliberately the *trap* estimate — sets up the reveal.
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
"$30M/yr ÷ 10% = $300M, premium only $120M → obviously yes." Deliberately the *trap* estimate — sets up the reveal.

steps.label: The hidden dis-synergies (F1).
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
# (a) Cannibalization: hot brand overlaps 20% of Fizzano's warm-season line
cannib = 150 * 0.20 * 0.30      # overlap_rev * overlap% * 30% CM = 9
# (b) Promo to hold combined shelf (Kellogg: promo-driven market)
promo = 7
# (c) Recurring route-retooling cost
route = 4
net_synergy = 30 - 9 - 7 - 4    # = 10
```

steps.label: Correct (net synergy value).
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
net_value = 10/0.10             # = 100  -> vs premium 120 -> OVERPAY by 20
```
**Flip: naive WORKS ($300M) → correct OVERPAY ($100M < $120M premium).** Overstatement **200%**.

steps.label: Sensitivity (one function — cannibalization overlap is the crux).
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
def net_value(overlap):
    return (30 - 150*overlap*0.30 - 7 - 4)/0.10
# 10% -> 145 (WORKS) | 20% -> 100 (OVERPAY) | 30% -> 55 (OVERPAY)
# breakeven overlap = 7/45 = 0.1556 -> below 15.6%, deal works
```
**Deal hinges on the overlap: measure it before signing.**

steps.label: Brainstorm
steps.candidate_md: (min 6, MECE — sources of dis-synergy): SKU cannibalization, shelf-facing loss to competitors, incremental trade promo, cold-vs-hot logistics incompatibility (different trucks), brand-dilution, sales-force retraining. (Revenue-erosion vs cost-add split.)
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
(min 6, MECE — sources of dis-synergy): SKU cannibalization, shelf-facing loss to competitors, incremental trade promo, cold-vs-hot logistics incompatibility (different trucks), brand-dilution, sales-force retraining. (Revenue-erosion vs cost-add split.)

steps.label: Recommendation (Pyramid).
steps.candidate_md:
*"Fizzano should not pay $120M — the deal overpays by $20M."* Facts: gross $30M synergy nets to only $10M after $9M cannibalization, $7M promo, $4M route cost; net value $100M < $120M premium; deal works only if brand overlap is below 15.6%. Risks: (internal) cannibalization estimate is soft — a 5-pt error swings $22M of value; (external) competitors respond to the shelf grab with promo, raising the $7M further. Next steps: 60-day overlap study on warm-season SKUs before signing; cap the premium at ≤$100M or index it to measured cannibalization.

**Six-check table (#74).**

| Check | Result | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|
| [1] Rounding chain | integer inputs, /0.10 once | 0.00 |
| [3] Overstatement base | vs final net $100M | 200.00% |
| [4] Gross-vs-net (the naive-error itself) | net = gross − Σ dis-synergy | correctly separated |
| [6] Metric unity | perpetuity /0.10 both | one formula ✓ |
| [9b] Sensitivity one fn | same `net_value()` | 0.00 |
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
*"Fizzano should not pay $120M — the deal overpays by $20M."* Facts: gross $30M synergy nets to only $10M after $9M cannibalization, $7M promo, $4M route cost; net value $100M < $120M premium; deal works only if brand overlap is below 15.6%. Risks: (internal) cannibalization estimate is soft — a 5-pt error swings $22M of value; (external) competitors respond to the shelf grab with promo, raising the $7M further. Next steps: 60-day overlap study on warm-season SKUs before signing; cap the premium at ≤$100M or index it to measured cannibalization.

**Six-check table (#74).**

| Check | Result | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|
| [1] Rounding chain | integer inputs, /0.10 once | 0.00 |
| [3] Overstatement base | vs final net $100M | 200.00% |
| [4] Gross-vs-net (the naive-error itself) | net = gross − Σ dis-synergy | correctly separated |
| [6] Metric unity | perpetuity /0.10 both | one formula ✓ |
| [9b] Sensitivity one fn | same `net_value()` | 0.00 |

cases.title: Trainspotting (Railcore Systems)
cases.header_md:
## Case 75 — Trainspotting (Railcore Systems) · Cost Reduction · Medium · Trap-flip · **open-drive**

> **ПЛАШКА** 
> **Тип:** Cost Reduction · **Индустрия:** Rail · **Формат:** —  
> **Difficulty:** 🟡 Medium · **Depth:** 🔥 STRONG · **Соответствие:** ↑выше  
> **T/C:** Trap · **Naive:** #14 fixed-cost allocation · **Время:** —  
> **Экспонаты (1):** table — Exhibit A — Plant P&L.  
> **Reveal:** on_request
> **optimal_entry:** 4  
>  
> **Слои insight:** ① F7 самоопровержение ② **#14 fixed-cost allocation** ③ sensitivity: при какой утилизации переворот

**Cost Reduction · Rail · Medium · Trap · naive #14 fixed-cost allocation · gen C**
*Registry: Trainspotting*

**Grounding (Stern Industrials p.22):** capital-intensive manufacturing (leased/owned); union labor; key metrics **capacity utilization** + **book-to-bill**; bundled aftermarket/maintenance. → Plant closure decisions must separate avoidable plant cost from unavoidable allocated corporate overhead.
cases.prompt_md:
Railcore Systems assembles rail equipment at three plants. **Plant C** reports the worst EBIT (occupancy only 50%). An analyst recommends closing it to cut costs. Should Railcore close Plant C? (WACC 10%.)

**Open-drive (after framework):** interviewer offers four directions — (a) plant utilization, (b) fixed/variable cost split, (c) **corporate-overhead allocation basis**, (d) aftermarket revenue by plant. *Optimal:* (c) first (surfaces the trap), then (b). *Red flag:* jumping to (a) and concluding "close the low-util plant" without checking (c).

exhibits.title: Exhibit A — Plant P&L.
exhibits.body_md:
**Exhibit A — Plant P&L.** (All contributions derive from one formula: `units × (price − variable cost) − plant fixed`. Note Plant C's higher variable cost — older, less-efficient plant.)

| Plant | Capacity | Util. | Units | Price/unit | Var cost/unit | Contribution margin | Plant fixed | Contribution (before corp OH) | Alloc. corp OH | Reported EBIT |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 1000 | 0.85 | 850 | 0.30 | 0.16 | 0.14 | 30 | 850×0.14−30 = **+89.00** | 25 | **+64.00** |
| B | 900 | 0.82 | 738 | 0.30 | 0.16 | 0.14 | 28 | 738×0.14−28 = **+75.32** | 25 | **+50.32** |
| C | 700 | 0.50 | 350 | 0.30 | **0.17** | **0.13** | 26 | 350×0.13−26 = **+19.50** | 25 | **−5.50** |

Corporate OH $75M ($25M each) is **unavoidable at group level** (HQ, R&D, brand). Every contribution above derives from the shown per-plant price/variable-cost/fixed — same formula for all three (exhibit-level metric unity).

steps.label: Framework.
steps.candidate_md: (separate avoidable plant cost from allocated corporate OH before deciding.)
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
(separate avoidable plant cost from allocated corporate OH before deciding.)

steps.label: Mental math (30–60s):
steps.candidate_md: Plant C contribution = 350 units × ($0.30−$0.17) − $26 fixed = 45.5 − 26 = **+$19.5M** *before* corporate OH. "Reported loss is just the $25M OH allocation — the plant itself contributes." Sign-check flips the answer before the model.
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
Plant C contribution = 350 units × ($0.30−$0.17) − $26 fixed = 45.5 − 26 = **+$19.5M** *before* corporate OH. "Reported loss is just the $25M OH allocation — the plant itself contributes." Sign-check flips the answer before the model.

steps.label: Naive (close on reported EBIT).
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
# analyst sees C at −5.5, assumes closing removes the loss (incl its $25M alloc OH)
naive_after = 108.82 - (-5.50)   # = 114.32   "improvement of +5.5" -> CLOSE
```

steps.label: Correct (#14 fixed-cost allocation).
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
# corp OH is unavoidable: C's $25M re-allocates to A&B. Only C's CONTRIBUTION is lost.
group_keep    = (89.00+75.32+19.50) - 75      # = 108.82
correct_after = 108.82 - 19.50                 # = 89.32   (KEEP: closing destroys 19.50)
```
**Flip: naive CLOSE (illusory +$5.5M) → correct KEEP (closing destroys $19.5M).** The "+$5.5M improvement" is exactly the allocated OH that doesn't actually leave (F7 self-contradiction).

steps.label: Sensitivity (one function — at what utilization is closing right?).
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
def c_contribution(util):
    return 700*util*(0.30-0.17) - 26
# 50% -> +19.50 (KEEP) | 40% -> +10.40 (KEEP) | 28% -> −0.52 (CLOSE)
# breakeven util = 26/(700*0.13) = 0.2857 -> below 28.6%, C fails to cover avoidable cost
```
**Close only when the plant stops covering its *avoidable* cost (28.6% util), not when reported EBIT turns negative.**

steps.label: Brainstorm
steps.candidate_md: (min 6 — before closing, what to check): reallocation impact on A&B reported margins, book-to-bill/backlog at C, aftermarket contracts tied to C, union severance + closure cost, customer geography served only by C, mothball vs sell vs close. (Cost-side vs strategic-side split.)
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
(min 6 — before closing, what to check): reallocation impact on A&B reported margins, book-to-bill/backlog at C, aftermarket contracts tied to C, union severance + closure cost, customer geography served only by C, mothball vs sell vs close. (Cost-side vs strategic-side split.)

steps.label: Recommendation (Pyramid).
steps.candidate_md:
*"Railcore should keep Plant C open."* Facts: C contributes +$19.5M before allocated overhead; its reported −$5.5M is entirely the $25M corporate-OH allocation, which re-allocates to A&B on closure; closing drops group EBIT from $108.8M to $89.3M. Risks: (internal) A&B margins will look worse post-reallocation and may trigger a second wrong closure; (external) at 50% utilization C is exposed if rail capex (book-to-bill) turns down. Next steps: monitor C's utilization against the 28.6% breakeven quarterly; pursue aftermarket contracts to lift C's contribution rather than close.

**Six-check table (#75).**

| Check | Result | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|
| Derive-first (ALL exhibit rows) | A/B/C contributions all derive from shown per-plant price/varcost/fixed | A 89.00, B 75.32, C 19.50 all reproduce |
| [1] Rounding chain | round once (B contrib 75.32 exact) | 0.00 |
| [14] Fixed-cost allocation (the naive-error) | corp OH unavoidable, re-allocates | correctly separated |
| [6] Metric unity | group EBIT one definition throughout | ✓ |
| [9b] Sensitivity one fn | same `c_contribution()` | 0.00 |
| Breakeven util | 26/(700·0.13) = 0.2857 | exact |
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
*"Railcore should keep Plant C open."* Facts: C contributes +$19.5M before allocated overhead; its reported −$5.5M is entirely the $25M corporate-OH allocation, which re-allocates to A&B on closure; closing drops group EBIT from $108.8M to $89.3M. Risks: (internal) A&B margins will look worse post-reallocation and may trigger a second wrong closure; (external) at 50% utilization C is exposed if rail capex (book-to-bill) turns down. Next steps: monitor C's utilization against the 28.6% breakeven quarterly; pursue aftermarket contracts to lift C's contribution rather than close.

**Six-check table (#75).**

| Check | Result | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|
| Derive-first (ALL exhibit rows) | A/B/C contributions all derive from shown per-plant price/varcost/fixed | A 89.00, B 75.32, C 19.50 all reproduce |
| [1] Rounding chain | round once (B contrib 75.32 exact) | 0.00 |
| [14] Fixed-cost allocation (the naive-error) | corp OH unavoidable, re-allocates | correctly separated |
| [6] Metric unity | group EBIT one definition throughout | ✓ |
| [9b] Sensitivity one fn | same `c_contribution()` | 0.00 |
| Breakeven util | 26/(700·0.13) = 0.2857 | exact |

cases.title: Here Comes the Sun (Rayldon Power)
cases.header_md:
## Case 76 — Here Comes the Sun (Rayldon Power) · Cost Reduction · **Easy** · Trap-action

> **ПЛАШКА** 
> **Тип:** Cost Reduction · **Индустрия:** Solar power · **Формат:** —  
> **Difficulty:** 🟢 Easy · **Depth:** ⚠️ STANDARD · **Соответствие:** ↑выше  
> **T/C:** Trap · **Naive:** #7 avg-vs-marginal · **Время:** —  
> **Экспонаты (1):** table — Exhibit A — Per-site O&M.  
> **Reveal:** on_request
> **optimal_entry:** 4  
>  
> **Слои insight:** ① решение по среднему ② **#7 avg-vs-marginal** → какие площадки пересекают порог вендора

**Cost Reduction · Solar power · Easy · Trap · naive #7 avg-vs-marginal · gen C**
*Registry: Here Comes the Sun*

**Grounding (Energy / solar):** solar O&M, panel degradation, capacity factor. **[DEVIATION flagged: no solar spread in Stern/Kellogg project files; nearest = Industrials capacity-utilization. Degradation/capacity-factor replaces book-to-bill. Named for reviewer.]**

**Trap subtype note.** This is **Trap-action**, not a direction flip: both paths reduce O&M cost, but the naive path picks a *suboptimal action* by deciding on the fleet **average** instead of **marginal** per-site cost. Counts as Trap for corridor accounting. (Deliberately not reverse-fit into a false flip — see process rule.)
cases.prompt_md: Rayldon Power runs a 5-site, 500MW solar fleet. A vendor offers to take over **all** O&M at a flat **$0.042M/MW** — below the fleet average of **$0.046M/MW**. Should Rayldon sign the fleet-wide deal? (Annual.)

steps.label: Framework.
steps.candidate_md: (compare vendor rate to what — average or per-site marginal?)
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
(compare vendor rate to what — average or per-site marginal?)

steps.label: Mental math (30–60s):
steps.candidate_md: "Vendor $0.042 vs average $0.046 → saves $0.004 × 500 = $2M." The trap estimate — it uses the average.
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
"Vendor $0.042 vs average $0.046 → saves $0.004 × 500 = $2M." The trap estimate — it uses the average.

steps.label: Naive (decide on average).
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
naive_saving = 23.0 - 0.042*500   # = 2.00  -> SIGN fleet-wide
```

steps.label: Correct (#7 avg-vs-marginal).
steps.candidate_md: Three of five sites already run **below** the vendor rate. Signing fleet-wide overpays on them. Outsource only where site cost > vendor.
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
correct_cost = sum((0.042*100 if om/100 > 0.042 else om) for om in [3.0,3.4,4.0,5.6,7.0])
# self-perform 1,2,3 (3.0+3.4+4.0) + outsource 4,5 (4.2+4.2) = 18.80
correct_saving = 23.0 - 18.80     # = 4.20  (more than double)
```
**Naive signs fleet-wide ($2.0M); correct outsources only the 2 degraded sites ($4.2M) — leaves $2.2M on the table.** The average masked that most sites are already cheaper than the vendor.

steps.label: Sensitivity (which sites cross the vendor line).
steps.candidate_md: Vendor $0.042 is the decision boundary: sites ≥$0.042/MW → outsource (4, 5); sites <$0.042 → self-perform (1, 2, 3). If the vendor rate fell to $0.038, site 3 ($0.040) would also cross → outsource 3, 4, 5.
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
Vendor $0.042 is the decision boundary: sites ≥$0.042/MW → outsource (4, 5); sites <$0.042 → self-perform (1, 2, 3). If the vendor rate fell to $0.038, site 3 ($0.040) would also cross → outsource 3, 4, 5.

steps.label: Brainstorm
steps.candidate_md: (min 6 — before signing anything): renegotiate a *tiered* contract (per-site rate), self-perform good sites, retire/repower the worst degraded sites instead of paying premium O&M, benchmark vendor SLA vs in-house uptime, check capacity-factor trajectory (degradation) per site, bundle only the high-cost sites. (Make vs buy vs retire.)
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
(min 6 — before signing anything): renegotiate a *tiered* contract (per-site rate), self-perform good sites, retire/repower the worst degraded sites instead of paying premium O&M, benchmark vendor SLA vs in-house uptime, check capacity-factor trajectory (degradation) per site, bundle only the high-cost sites. (Make vs buy vs retire.)

steps.label: Recommendation (Pyramid).
steps.candidate_md:
*"Rayldon should reject the fleet-wide deal and outsource only Sunfield 4 and 5."* Facts: three sites already run below the vendor's $0.042/MW, so a fleet deal overpays on them; selective outsourcing saves $4.2M vs $2.0M for the fleet deal; the fleet average hid the per-site spread. Risks: (internal) splitting O&M raises coordination overhead across two providers; (external) vendor may refuse a 2-site-only contract or reprice it higher. Next steps: request a per-site quote for Sunfield 4–5 within 30 days; commission a degradation study to decide repower-vs-outsource on Sunfield 5.

**Six-check table (#76).**

| Check | Result | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|
| [7] Avg-vs-marginal (the naive-error) | decision on per-site marginal, not fleet avg | correctly separated |
| [1] Rounding chain | integer/2dp inputs | 0.00 |
| Trap-action tag honesty | both paths save; naive suboptimal (not reverse-fit to flip) | intentional |
| [6] Metric unity | O&M cost per MW, one definition | ✓ |
| Corridor accounting | counts as Trap (not Clean) | Clean stays 13.8% |
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
*"Rayldon should reject the fleet-wide deal and outsource only Sunfield 4 and 5."* Facts: three sites already run below the vendor's $0.042/MW, so a fleet deal overpays on them; selective outsourcing saves $4.2M vs $2.0M for the fleet deal; the fleet average hid the per-site spread. Risks: (internal) splitting O&M raises coordination overhead across two providers; (external) vendor may refuse a 2-site-only contract or reprice it higher. Next steps: request a per-site quote for Sunfield 4–5 within 30 days; commission a degradation study to decide repower-vs-outsource on Sunfield 5.

**Six-check table (#76).**

| Check | Result | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|
| [7] Avg-vs-marginal (the naive-error) | decision on per-site marginal, not fleet avg | correctly separated |
| [1] Rounding chain | integer/2dp inputs | 0.00 |
| Trap-action tag honesty | both paths save; naive suboptimal (not reverse-fit to flip) | intentional |
| [6] Metric unity | O&M cost per MW, one definition | ✓ |
| Corridor accounting | counts as Trap (not Clean) | Clean stays 13.8% |

cases.title: The Shocking Truth (Gridholm Power)
cases.header_md:
## Case 77 — The Shocking Truth (Gridholm Power) · Pricing · Medium · Trap-flip · hypothesis-update

> **ПЛАШКА** 
> **Тип:** Pricing · **Индустрия:** Utility · **Формат:** —  
> **Difficulty:** 🟡 Medium · **Depth:** 🔥 STRONG · **Соответствие:** ↑выше  
> **T/C:** Trap · **Naive:** #12 regulator reaction · **Время:** —  
> **Экспонаты (2):** table — Exhibit A — Regulated economics.; table — Exhibit B — the real lever.  
> **Reveal:** auto, on_request
> **optimal_entry:** 4  
>  
> **Слои insight:** ① full flow-through naive ② hypothesis-update ③ **#12 regulatory clawback**

**Pricing · Utility · Medium · Trap · naive #12 regulator reaction · gen C**
*Registry: The Shocking Truth*

**Grounding (regulated electric utility).** No direct spread in project files → grounded on the **regulated-return framework**: profit is capped at *allowed ROE × rate base*, and earnings above the cap are refunded (clawback). **[DEVIATION NAMED: rate-base / allowed-return economics replace a casebook spread; the mechanic is REGULATORY CLAWBACK, not competitive response. Flag to reviewer.]**
cases.prompt_md: Gridholm Power, a regulated utility, proposes a **12% rate increase** (from $100 to $112/MWh) to lift revenue by $240M. Management projects the full $240M drops to profit. Should Gridholm expect $240M more profit? (Rate base $4,000M, allowed ROE 9%.)

exhibits.title: Exhibit A — Regulated economics.
exhibits.body_md:
**Exhibit A — Regulated economics.**

| Item | Value | Derivation |
|---|---|---|
| Rate base | $4,000M | regulated asset value |
| Allowed ROE | 9% | regulator-set |
| **Allowed profit (ceiling)** | **$360M** | 4,000 × 0.09 |
| Volume | 20,000 GWh | — |
| Current rate | $100/MWh | → revenue $2,000M |
| Opex | $1,640M | — |
| **Current profit** | **$360M** | 2,000 − 1,640 = *exactly at the allowed ceiling* |

exhibits.title: Exhibit B — the real lever.
exhibits.body_md:
**Exhibit B — the real lever.** The regulator allows 9% on **new** rate base. The uplift comes from *rate-base growth* (grid capex), not from price.
```python
def profit_uplift(new_rate, capex):   # ONE function, both levers
    raw = 20000*1000*new_rate/1e6 - 1640
    allowed = (4000+capex)*0.09
    return min(raw, allowed) - 360
# hike only  (112, 0)    -> 0    (refunded)
# capex only (100, 1000) -> 0    (ceiling rises to 450 but revenue still at old rate -> raw 360)
# BOTH       (112, 1000) -> +90  (raw 600 capped at new allowed 450)
# to capture the full $240 -> capex = 600/0.09 - 4000 = $2,667M
```
**Both levers are required; neither alone moves profit.** This is the genuine regulated mechanic.

steps.label: Framework.
steps.candidate_md: (does the rate hike flow to profit, or is it capped? what lifts the ceiling?)
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
(does the rate hike flow to profit, or is it capped? what lifts the ceiling?)

steps.label: Naive (full flow-through).
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
new_revenue = 20000*1000*112/1e6      # = 2240
naive_profit = 2240 - 1640            # = 600
naive_uplift = 600 - 360              # = +240  -> "rate hike = +$240M profit"
```

steps.label: Mental math (30–60s):
steps.candidate_md: "Current profit $360M already equals allowed 9% × $4,000M. Regulator won't let profit exceed the cap." Sign-check kills the naive answer before the model.
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
"Current profit $360M already equals allowed 9% × $4,000M. Regulator won't let profit exceed the cap." Sign-check kills the naive answer before the model.

steps.label: Hypothesis-update (before Exhibit B):
steps.candidate_md: *what happens to the extra $240M?* Strong hypothesis: it's refunded. (Exhibit B confirms the clawback — and partly breaks the "hike is useless" conclusion: paired with capex it DOES lift profit.)
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
*what happens to the extra $240M?* Strong hypothesis: it's refunded. (Exhibit B confirms the clawback — and partly breaks the "hike is useless" conclusion: paired with capex it DOES lift profit.)

steps.label: Correct (#12 regulatory clawback).
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
allowed_profit = 4000*0.09            # = 360 (the ceiling)
correct_profit = min(600, 360)        # = 360
clawback = 600 - 360                  # = 240 refunded to ratepayers
correct_uplift = 360 - 360            # = 0  -> the hike ALONE earns nothing
```
**Flip: naive +$240M → correct $0** (entire uplift refunded).

steps.label: Sensitivity
steps.candidate_md: (above). The crux is the capex paired with the hike: a realistic $1,000M grid-modernization program earns +$90M; full capture needs $2,667M.
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
(above). The crux is the capex paired with the hike: a realistic $1,000M grid-modernization program earns +$90M; full capture needs $2,667M.

steps.label: Brainstorm
steps.candidate_md: (min 6, MECE — ways to legitimately lift regulated profit): rate-base growth (capex), approved cost-of-capital increase, performance incentive mechanisms, non-regulated adjacent services, opex-efficiency sharing bands, decoupling riders. (Regulated levers vs unregulated adjacencies.)
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
(min 6, MECE — ways to legitimately lift regulated profit): rate-base growth (capex), approved cost-of-capital increase, performance incentive mechanisms, non-regulated adjacent services, opex-efficiency sharing bands, decoupling riders. (Regulated levers vs unregulated adjacencies.)

steps.label: Recommendation (Pyramid).
steps.candidate_md:
*"Gridholm should not expect $240M from the rate hike — the hike alone earns $0; the profit lever is rate-base growth."* Facts: current profit already sits at the $360M allowed ceiling; a hike's excess is fully refunded via clawback; a $1,000M grid-modernization capex, paired with cost recovery, earns +$90M at the allowed 9%. Risks: (internal) capex program execution/overrun risk; (external/regulatory) the regulator may disallow part of the capex from rate base. Next steps: file a rate case pairing the capex with cost recovery within the next cycle; model the allowed-return sensitivity to a possible ROE cut.

**Six-check table (#77).**

| Check | Result | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|
| Derive-first | all exhibit rows from rate base × ROE | allowed profit 360 = 4000×0.09 |
| [1] Rounding chain | round once | 0.00 |
| [6] Metric unity | `profit_uplift()` one function, both levers | ✓ |
| [9b] Sensitivity one fn | same function, args only | 0.00 |
| Flip integrity | naive 240 → correct 0 (clawback) | direction flip real |
| Interaction check | capex-only AND hike-only both = 0; both together = 90 | verified logic |
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
*"Gridholm should not expect $240M from the rate hike — the hike alone earns $0; the profit lever is rate-base growth."* Facts: current profit already sits at the $360M allowed ceiling; a hike's excess is fully refunded via clawback; a $1,000M grid-modernization capex, paired with cost recovery, earns +$90M at the allowed 9%. Risks: (internal) capex program execution/overrun risk; (external/regulatory) the regulator may disallow part of the capex from rate base. Next steps: file a rate case pairing the capex with cost recovery within the next cycle; model the allowed-return sensitivity to a possible ROE cut.

**Six-check table (#77).**

| Check | Result | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|
| Derive-first | all exhibit rows from rate base × ROE | allowed profit 360 = 4000×0.09 |
| [1] Rounding chain | round once | 0.00 |
| [6] Metric unity | `profit_uplift()` one function, both levers | ✓ |
| [9b] Sensitivity one fn | same function, args only | 0.00 |
| Flip integrity | naive 240 → correct 0 (clawback) | direction flip real |
| Interaction check | capex-only AND hike-only both = 0; both together = 90 | verified logic |

cases.title: The Grand Budapest Hotel (Staymore Hotels)
cases.header_md:
## Case 78 — The Grand Budapest Hotel (Staymore Hotels) · Pricing · **Easy** · Trap-flip

> **ПЛАШКА** 
> **Тип:** Pricing · **Индустрия:** Hotels · **Формат:** —  
> **Difficulty:** 🟢 Easy · **Depth:** ⚠️ STANDARD · **Соответствие:** ↑выше  
> **T/C:** Trap · **Naive:** #11 expected value · **Время:** —  
> **Экспонаты (1):** table — Exhibit A — No-show distribution (nightly).  
> **Reveal:** on_request
> **optimal_entry:** 4  
>  
> **Слои insight:** ① overbook = no-show rate (naive) ② **#11 expected value**, walk cost — суть

**Pricing · Hotels · Easy · Trap · naive #11 expected value · gen C**
*Registry: The Grand Budapest Hotel*

**Setting:** Central/Eastern Europe; Staymore's **Budapest flagship** (300 rooms). *(Title dictates setting — a "Grand Budapest Hotel" case is set in Budapest, not Miami.)*

**Grounding (Kellogg Hospitality Co p.13):** rooms × occupancy × days; fixed + variable cost per night; business-traveler clientele; the room-night is *perishable inventory*.
cases.prompt_md: Staymore's Budapest flagship runs an 8% no-show rate. Revenue management proposes **overbooking by 8% (24 rooms)** to offset no-shows and capture ~$4,320/night in otherwise-lost revenue. Is that the right overbooking level? (ADR $180; walking a guest costs $2,200 — competitor's premium rate + upgrade comp + loyalty damage.)

exhibits.title: Exhibit A — No-show distribution (nightly).
exhibits.body_md:
**Exhibit A — No-show distribution (nightly).**

| No-shows | 15 | 20 | 24 | 28 | 33 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Probability | 0.15 | 0.25 | 0.30 | 0.20 | 0.10 |

Expected no-shows ≈ **23** (~8% of 300).

steps.label: Framework.
steps.candidate_md: (overbooking is an expected-value tradeoff: extra revenue vs walk cost.)
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
(overbooking is an expected-value tradeoff: extra revenue vs walk cost.)

steps.label: Naive (overbook = no-show rate).
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
naive_overbook = 300*0.08          # = 24 rooms
naive_revenue  = 24*180            # = $4,320/night  "free money" (assumes exactly 8% no-show)
```

steps.label: Mental math (30–60s):
steps.candidate_md: "No-shows *vary* — some nights only 15. Overbook 24 and on a 15-no-show night I walk 9 guests at $2,200 = $19,800 hit." The variance is the whole game; the point estimate is a trap.
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
"No-shows *vary* — some nights only 15. Overbook 24 and on a 15-no-show night I walk 9 guests at $2,200 = $19,800 hit." The variance is the whole game; the point estimate is a trap.

steps.label: Correct (#11 expected value).
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
scenarios = {15:0.15, 20:0.25, 24:0.30, 28:0.20, 33:0.10}
def ev(N):   # overbook N rooms
    return sum(p*(min(N,s)*180 - max(0,N-s)*2200) for s,p in scenarios.items())
# EV(15)=+2700 | EV(20)=+1815 | EV(24)=−1273 (LOSS) | EV(28)=−7217
# optimal overbook = 15 rooms (EV +$2,700/night)
```
**Flip: naive "overbook 24 = +$4,320 free" → correct "24 LOSES $1,273/night; optimal is 15."** The naive level ignores walk cost entirely; at 24 rooms the high-show scenarios turn it negative.

steps.label: Sensitivity (walk cost is the crux).
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
# walk $900 -> optimal 20 | walk $1,500 -> optimal 15 | walk $2,200 -> optimal 15, naive-24 negative
```
Higher walk cost → lower optimal overbook. Annual (350 nights): optimal +$0.945M vs naive −$0.446M.

steps.label: Brainstorm
steps.candidate_md: (min 6 — levers beyond overbooking): tiered deposit/prepay to cut no-shows, waitlist/standby fill, dynamic walk-cost partnerships with nearby hotels, no-show fee, corporate-contract guaranteed rooms, same-day discount to fill. (Reduce-no-shows vs manage-the-walk-risk.)
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
(min 6 — levers beyond overbooking): tiered deposit/prepay to cut no-shows, waitlist/standby fill, dynamic walk-cost partnerships with nearby hotels, no-show fee, corporate-contract guaranteed rooms, same-day discount to fill. (Reduce-no-shows vs manage-the-walk-risk.)

steps.label: Recommendation (Pyramid).
steps.candidate_md:
*"Staymore should overbook by 15 rooms, not 24."* Facts: at $2,200 walk cost, overbooking 24 loses $1,273/night because no-shows vary below 24 on ~40% of nights; 15 rooms maximizes EV at +$2,700/night; the naive "$4,320 free" ignores walk cost entirely. Risks: (internal) walk events damage loyalty beyond the modeled cost; (external) a competitor's rate spike raises the walk cost and lowers the optimal further. Next steps: implement 15-room overbooking with a real-time walk-cost feed; pilot prepay deposits to cut the no-show rate itself.

**Six-check table (#78).**

| Check | Result | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|
| Derive-first | EV from shown scenario distribution | probs sum to 1.00 |
| [11] Expected value (naive-error) | EV over distribution, not point estimate | correctly separated |
| [1] Rounding chain | round once | 0.00 |
| [9b] Sensitivity one fn | same `ev()` | 0.00 |
| Flip integrity | naive +4,320 → correct 24-room EV −1,273 | direction flip real |
| Setting consistency | Budapest flagship per title | ✓ |
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
*"Staymore should overbook by 15 rooms, not 24."* Facts: at $2,200 walk cost, overbooking 24 loses $1,273/night because no-shows vary below 24 on ~40% of nights; 15 rooms maximizes EV at +$2,700/night; the naive "$4,320 free" ignores walk cost entirely. Risks: (internal) walk events damage loyalty beyond the modeled cost; (external) a competitor's rate spike raises the walk cost and lowers the optimal further. Next steps: implement 15-room overbooking with a real-time walk-cost feed; pilot prepay deposits to cut the no-show rate itself.

**Six-check table (#78).**

| Check | Result | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|
| Derive-first | EV from shown scenario distribution | probs sum to 1.00 |
| [11] Expected value (naive-error) | EV over distribution, not point estimate | correctly separated |
| [1] Rounding chain | round once | 0.00 |
| [9b] Sensitivity one fn | same `ev()` | 0.00 |
| Flip integrity | naive +4,320 → correct 24-room EV −1,273 | direction flip real |
| Setting consistency | Budapest flagship per title | ✓ |

cases.title: Between a Rock and a Hard Place (Quarrix Materials)
cases.title_registry: Between a Rock and a Hard Place
cases.header_md:
## Case 79 — Between a Rock and a Hard Place (Quarrix Materials) · Market Entry · Medium · **Clean**

> **ПЛАШКА** 
> **Тип:** Market Entry · **Индустрия:** Aggregates · **Формат:** —  
> **Difficulty:** 🟡 Medium · **Depth:** 🔥 STRONG · **Соответствие:** ↑выше  
> **T/C:** Clean · **Naive:** — · **Время:** —  
> **Экспонаты (1):** table — Exhibit A — Entry economics (all derived).  
> **Reveal:** on_request
> **optimal_entry:** 3  
>  
> **Слои insight:** ① clean-проверки (есть ли скрытая freight-ловушка) ② **CLEAN**: correct=naive=ENTER ③ sensitivity «что БЫ перевернуло» — freight breakeven $5/т

**Market Entry · Aggregates · Medium · Clean · gen C**
*Registry: Between a Rock and a Hard Place*

**Grounding (Stern Industrials p.22):** capacity utilization; aggregates are heavy/low-value → economically freight-bound (~50-mile radius). **[DEVIATION NAMED: aggregates ≈ Industrials, but FREIGHT RADIUS replaces book-to-bill as the market-capping metric. Flag to reviewer.]**

**Clean-case purpose.** A trap-trained candidate will *hunt* for a freight/capacity death-trap (a #6 capacity reversal) and want to reject entry. Correct: the freight radius genuinely limits the market, but the economics still clear the hurdle → **ENTER**. Validate that no hidden capacity/freight killer exists.
cases.prompt_md: Quarrix Materials (aggregates) considers entering a new metro by building a quarry+plant. Demand is 5,000 kt/yr; Quarrix can win ~60%. Should it enter? (Price $14/ton, production $7, freight $2; capex $30M, plant capacity 3,500 kt, fixed $3M/yr, WACC 10%.)

exhibits.title: Exhibit A — Entry economics (all derived).
exhibits.body_md:
**Exhibit A — Entry economics (all derived).**

| Item | Value | Derivation |
|---|---|---|
| Metro demand | 5,000 kt/yr | — |
| Winnable @60% | 3,000 kt | 5,000 × 0.60 |
| Plant capacity | 3,500 kt | > winnable → not binding |
| Volume served | 3,000 kt | min(3,000, 3,500) |
| Contribution/ton | $5 | 14 − 7 − 2 |
| Annual contribution | $15M | 3,000 kt × $5/ton |
| Fixed | $3M | — |
| **Annual profit** | **$12M** | 15 − 3 |

steps.label: Framework.
steps.candidate_md: (size the freight-bound market; check capacity bind; NPV vs capex.)
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
(size the freight-bound market; check capacity bind; NPV vs capex.)

steps.label: Mental math (30–60s):
steps.candidate_md: "3,000 kt × $5 = $15M contribution, less $3M fixed = $12M/yr → perpetuity $120M against $30M capex. Clearly positive unless something caps it." Then hunt for the cap.
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
"3,000 kt × $5 = $15M contribution, less $3M fixed = $12M/yr → perpetuity $120M against $30M capex. Clearly positive unless something caps it." Then hunt for the cap.

steps.label: The clean checks (is there a hidden freight/capacity killer?).
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
# capacity: winnable 3,000 kt < plant capacity 3,500 kt -> NOT binding, no lost upside
# freight: $2/ton leaves contribution $5/ton POSITIVE -> no margin kill
# NPV = 12/0.10 - 30 = +90  -> ENTER
```

steps.label: Correct = naive = ENTER.
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
npv = 12/0.10 - 30    # = +90   (both paths agree)
```
No reversal to invent. The freight radius is real but does not bite at these economics.

steps.label: Sensitivity (what WOULD flip it — clean confirmation).
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
def npv79(share, freight=2, capex=30):
    vol = min(5000*share, 3500)
    return (vol*(14-7-freight)/1000 - 3)/0.10 - capex
# freight breakeven = $5.00/ton (above this, contribution too thin -> entry flips to NO)
# breakeven share ~ 0.24 (base 0.60 is 150% above)
```
Entry survives up to $5/ton freight (base $2) — robustly positive. The candidate confirms the feared freight/capacity trap does not materialize.

steps.label: Brainstorm
steps.candidate_md: (min 6 — confirm no hidden killer before committing): verify the 50-mile freight radius covers the demand centers, confirm incumbent can't undercut on freight from a closer pit, check permitting/environmental risk on the quarry, stress the 60% winnable share, confirm reserve life of the deposit, check cyclicality (Industrials tied to GDP/construction). (Confirm-clean checklist.)
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
(min 6 — confirm no hidden killer before committing): verify the 50-mile freight radius covers the demand centers, confirm incumbent can't undercut on freight from a closer pit, check permitting/environmental risk on the quarry, stress the 60% winnable share, confirm reserve life of the deposit, check cyclicality (Industrials tied to GDP/construction). (Confirm-clean checklist.)

steps.label: Recommendation (Pyramid).
steps.candidate_md:
*"Quarrix should enter the metro — NPV +$90M, and the feared freight/capacity trap does not bite."* Facts: at $5/ton contribution and 3,000 kt winnable, annual profit is $12M against $30M capex; plant capacity (3,500 kt) exceeds winnable volume so nothing is stranded; entry survives freight up to $5/ton. Risks: (internal) reserve life and permitting timeline; (external) an incumbent with a closer pit could win the freight-cost battle in contested zones. Next steps: confirm the deposit's reserve life and haul-radius coverage of demand centers before capex commitment.

**Six-check table (#79).**

| Check | Result | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|
| Derive-first (ALL rows) | volume/contribution/profit all from shown price/cost/freight | $12M reproduces |
| [1] Rounding chain | round once | 0.00 |
| Clean = naive==correct | +90 == +90 | 0.00% |
| Capacity check | winnable 3,000 < capacity 3,500 | not binding |
| [9b] Sensitivity one fn | same `npv79()` | freight breakeven $5.00 |
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
*"Quarrix should enter the metro — NPV +$90M, and the feared freight/capacity trap does not bite."* Facts: at $5/ton contribution and 3,000 kt winnable, annual profit is $12M against $30M capex; plant capacity (3,500 kt) exceeds winnable volume so nothing is stranded; entry survives freight up to $5/ton. Risks: (internal) reserve life and permitting timeline; (external) an incumbent with a closer pit could win the freight-cost battle in contested zones. Next steps: confirm the deposit's reserve life and haul-radius coverage of demand centers before capex commitment.

**Six-check table (#79).**

| Check | Result | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|
| Derive-first (ALL rows) | volume/contribution/profit all from shown price/cost/freight | $12M reproduces |
| [1] Rounding chain | round once | 0.00 |
| Clean = naive==correct | +90 == +90 | 0.00% |
| Capacity check | winnable 3,000 < capacity 3,500 | not binding |
| [9b] Sensitivity one fn | same `npv79()` | freight breakeven $5.00 |

cases.title: Drive (Ridemos)
cases.header_md:
## Case 80 — Drive (Ridemos) · Market Entry · Medium · **Clean** · hypothesis-update + open-drive

> **ПЛАШКА** 
> **Тип:** Market Entry · **Индустрия:** Ride-hail · **Формат:** —  
> **Difficulty:** 🟡 Medium · **Depth:** 🔥 STRONG · **Соответствие:** ↑выше  
> **T/C:** Clean · **Naive:** — · **Время:** —  
> **Экспонаты (1):** table — Exhibit A — Entry economics (all derived).  
> **Reveal:** on_request
> **optimal_entry:** 3  
>  
> **Слои insight:** ① open-drive ② hypothesis-update ③ **CLEAN**: проверка, кусается ли liquidity floor ④ «когда БЫ укусил»

**Market Entry · Ride-hail · Medium · Clean · gen C**
*Registry: Drive*

**Grounding (Stern Technology p.15):** network effects, take rate. **[DEVIATION NAMED: two-sided marketplace — driver-supply LIQUIDITY replaces classic capacity as the binding concern. Flag to reviewer.]**

**Clean-case purpose (distinct mechanic from #79).** A trap-trained candidate fears a *liquidity death-spiral* (too few drivers → long waits → rider churn → fewer drivers) and wants to reject. Correct: at this niche's volume the driver economics genuinely clear the coverage SLA, so the feared liquidity floor does **not** bite → **ENTER**. Validate no false reversal.

> **Author's note for reviewer:** #80 was originally planned as a Trap-flip (#6 liquidity). After repeated honest derive-first attempts, the liquidity mechanic on realistic marketplace economics produced an *overstate*, not a direction flip, at every plausible calibration — forcing a flip required contorting the numbers (violating the no-reverse-fit rule). Per the locked process rule ("if a mechanic refuses to flip after honest attempts, let it be clean/overstate rather than contort"), #80 is delivered as **Clean**. Batch subtype impact: 5 flip / 2 soft / 3 clean; soft-traps stay at the ≤2 limit; cumulative Clean 12/80 = 15.0% (top of corridor — **note for #81+: hold 0–1 clean next batch**).
cases.prompt_md: Ridemos, a regional ride-hail player, considers entering a mid-size city with a **premium/airport** service. Demand is 5,000 premium riders × 3 trips/month at $45. Should it enter? (Take rate 25%; to hold the premium <10-min-pickup SLA, ~150 drivers must be on-call across coverage zones, guaranteed $2,500/month; entry cost $4M; WACC 10%.)

exhibits.title: Exhibit A — Entry economics (all derived).
exhibits.body_md:
**Exhibit A — Entry economics (all derived).**

| Item | Value | Derivation |
|---|---|---|
| Riders | 5,000 | premium niche |
| Trips/rider/mo | 3 | — |
| Demand trips | 15,000/mo | 5,000 × 3 |
| Fare | $45 | premium |
| Take rate | 25% | → $11.25/trip to platform |
| Annual revenue | $2.03M | 15,000 × 45 × 0.25 × 12 |
| Ops | $1.2M | — |
| **Annual profit** | **$0.83M** | 2.03 − 1.20 |

steps.label: Framework + open-drive.
steps.candidate_md: After framework, interviewer names four directions — (a) rider demand, (b) driver supply/liquidity, (c) unit economics per trip, (d) incumbent presence. *Optimal:* (b) liquidity first (the marketplace's binding concern), then (c). *Red flag:* sizing (a) demand and concluding ENTER without ever checking (b).
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
After framework, interviewer names four directions — (a) rider demand, (b) driver supply/liquidity, (c) unit economics per trip, (d) incumbent presence. *Optimal:* (b) liquidity first (the marketplace's binding concern), then (c). *Red flag:* sizing (a) demand and concluding ENTER without ever checking (b).

steps.label: Hypothesis-update (before the driver exhibit):
steps.candidate_md: *what will driver economics show?* Strong hypothesis: a liquidity floor cost will erode profit. (The exhibit partly confirms the concern is worth checking — and breaks it: the floor doesn't actually bite at this volume.)
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
*what will driver economics show?* Strong hypothesis: a liquidity floor cost will erode profit. (The exhibit partly confirms the concern is worth checking — and breaks it: the floor doesn't actually bite at this volume.)

steps.label: The clean check (does the liquidity floor bite?).
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
trips_per_driver = 15000/150            # = 100/month
driver_earnings  = 100 * 45 * 0.75      # = $3,375/month (drivers keep 75% of fare)
guarantee        = 2500
topup = max(0, guarantee - driver_earnings)   # = max(0, 2500-3375) = $0
# drivers self-sustain on trip earnings -> NO liquidity subsidy needed -> feared floor doesn't bite
```

steps.label: Correct = naive = ENTER.
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
profit = 2.03 - 1.20        # = 0.83
npv = 0.83/0.10 - 4         # = +4.25   (106% over the $4M entry cost)
```
No reversal to invent. The liquidity concern is real to *check* but does not materialize.

steps.label: Sensitivity (when WOULD the floor bite — clean confirmation).
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
# TWO distinct thresholds (keep them separate):
#  (1) topup STARTS: driver earnings = guarantee -> trips/driver = 2500/(45*0.75) = 74.07
#      -> riders = 74.07 * 150 / 3 = 3,704 riders. Below this, the subsidy kicks in.
#  (2) DECISION reverses (NPV=0): at ~3,951 riders. Between 3,704 and 3,951 the subsidy
#      bites but entry is still NPV-positive; only below ~3,951 does entry flip to NO.
# base 5,000 riders > both thresholds -> robust ENTER
```
At 3,000 riders NPV is negative (subsidy dominates); at 3,704 the topup just begins; at ~3,951 NPV crosses zero; at 4,000+ NPV is positive and topup is $0. Base 5,000 clears both thresholds comfortably. **Note the ~25% margin to reversal (5,000 vs 3,951) — this is a more borderline clean than #79's freight margin (150%).**

steps.label: Brainstorm
steps.candidate_md: (min 6 — confirm liquidity holds before entry): verify 150 drivers can be recruited without subsidy, confirm airport permit/access, check incumbent's driver lock-in, stress the 3-trip/rider frequency, model surge-hour coverage gaps, confirm the <10-min SLA is achievable with 150 across zones. (Confirm-liquidity checklist.)
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
(min 6 — confirm liquidity holds before entry): verify 150 drivers can be recruited without subsidy, confirm airport permit/access, check incumbent's driver lock-in, stress the 3-trip/rider frequency, model surge-hour coverage gaps, confirm the <10-min SLA is achievable with 150 across zones. (Confirm-liquidity checklist.)

steps.label: Recommendation (Pyramid).
steps.candidate_md:
*"Ridemos should enter the premium niche — NPV +$4.25M, and the feared driver-liquidity floor does not bite."* Facts: at 100 trips/driver, drivers earn $3,375/month against a $2,500 guarantee, so no subsidy is needed; annual profit $0.83M gives NPV +$4.25M over the $4M entry; the liquidity floor starts biting below 3,704 riders and the decision only reverses below ~3,951 riders (base 5,000). Risks: (internal) driver recruitment may lag the SLA in early months; (external) an incumbent could lock up drivers or airport access. Next steps: pre-sign 150 drivers before launch; secure airport permits and model surge-hour coverage.

**Six-check table (#80).**

| Check | Result | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|
| Derive-first (ALL rows) | revenue/profit/driver-earnings from shown inputs | $0.83M, $3,375 reproduce |
| [1] Rounding chain | round once | 0.00 |
| Clean = naive==correct | +4.25 == +4.25 | 0.00% |
| Liquidity check | driver earn $3,375 > guarantee $2,500 → topup $0 | floor non-binding |
| [9b] Sensitivity one fn | breakeven riders 3,704 | derived |

---

cases.title: Fight Club (Reptrix)
cases.header_md:
## Case 81 — Fight Club (Reptrix) · Pricing · **Medium** · Trap-flip · hypothesis-update

> **ПЛАШКА** 
> **Тип:** Pricing · **Индустрия:** Fitness · **Формат:** F1  
> **Difficulty:** 🟡 Medium · **Depth:** 🔥 STRONG · **Соответствие:** ↑выше  
> **T/C:** Trap · **Naive:** #10 cannibalization · **Время:** —  
> **Экспонаты (1):** table — Exhibit — Catchment study.  
> **Reveal:** auto
> **optimal_entry:** 4  
>  
> **Слои insight:** ① naive revenue ② hypothesis-update ③ каннибализация

**Pricing · Fitness · Medium · Trap · naive #10 cannibalization · gen C**
*Registry: Fight Club*

**Pricing · Fitness · F1 · Ловушка #10 (cannibalization)**
*Grounding: Retail p.13 recurring-traffic — [DEVIATION NAMED: membership subscription + catchment cannibalization замещает in-store footfall].*
cases.prompt_md: Reptrix, a fitness-club chain, wants to open a **new club** in a growing district, 2 miles from an existing Reptrix club. The new site projects **3,000 members** at **$40/month**. Fixed cost of the new club is **$0.9M/yr**. Should Reptrix open it? (WACC 10%, perpetuity.)

exhibits.title: Exhibit — Catchment study.
exhibits.body_md: **Exhibit — Catchment study.** Of the 3,000 projected members, **45% are transfers** from the existing Reptrix club 2 miles away (same catchment). The existing club loses those members' revenue.

steps.label: Framework (open-drive-lite).
steps.candidate_md: Candidate structures: incremental revenue vs incremental cost, and critically **whether the new members are truly new** (catchment overlap). Flag if candidate treats all 3,000 as incremental without questioning the nearby club.
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
Candidate structures: incremental revenue vs incremental cost, and critically **whether the new members are truly new** (catchment overlap). Flag if candidate treats all 3,000 as incremental without questioning the nearby club.

steps.label: Naive revenue.
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
from decimal import Decimal as D, ROUND_HALF_UP
def q(x,n=0): return D(str(x)).quantize(D('1.'+'0'*n) if n else D('1'),rounding=ROUND_HALF_UP)
WACC=D('0.10')
new_members=D('3000'); annual_fee=D('40')*12   # $480/yr
gross_rev=new_members*annual_fee/D('1000000')  # $1.44M
fixed=D('0.9')
naive_profit=gross_rev-fixed                   # 0.54
naive_value=naive_profit/WACC                  # 5.4
```
Naive: gross revenue **$1.44M/yr**, minus fixed **$0.9M** = **$0.54M/yr** → value **$5.4M** → looks like a clear OPEN.

steps.label: Mental-math check.
steps.candidate_md: 3,000 × $480 ≈ 3,000 × $500 = $1.5M gross; minus ~$0.9M ≈ $0.6M/yr. Positive at first glance — *"прикинь устно, точный расчёт — после"*.
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
3,000 × $480 ≈ 3,000 × $500 = $1.5M gross; minus ~$0.9M ≈ $0.6M/yr. Positive at first glance — *"прикинь устно, точный расчёт — после"*.

steps.label: Hypothesis-update.
steps.candidate_md: Interviewer: *"Before we look at the catchment data — what share of the 3,000 do you expect are genuinely new vs. poached from the nearby club?"* Strong hypothesis: meaningful overlap given 2-mile distance. Weak: assume all new.
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
Interviewer: *"Before we look at the catchment data — what share of the 3,000 do you expect are genuinely new vs. poached from the nearby club?"* Strong hypothesis: meaningful overlap given 2-mile distance. Weak: assume all new.

steps.label: Correct (cannibalization).
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
transfer_share=D('0.45')
transfers=new_members*transfer_share           # 1350
lost_rev=transfers*annual_fee/D('1000000')     # 0.648  (revenue already ours)
true_incremental_rev=gross_rev-lost_rev        # 0.792
correct_profit=true_incremental_rev-fixed      # -0.108
correct_value=correct_profit/WACC              # -1.08
```
Correct: only **55% (1,650 members)** are truly new. True incremental revenue = **$0.792M**, minus fixed **$0.9M** = **−$0.108M/yr** → value **−$1.08M** → **DO NOT OPEN**.

steps.label: Sensitivity (one function).
steps.interviewer_md:
```python
def value_at(share):
    lost=new_members*share*annual_fee/D('1000000')
    return ((gross_rev-lost)-fixed)/WACC
# breakeven transfer share:
breakeven = 1 - fixed/gross_rev                 # 0.375
```
Breakeven transfer share = **37.5%**. Below that the club adds value; the actual 45% puts it underwater. At 30% transfers value would be **+$0.6M** (open); at 50%, **−$1.8M**.

steps.label: Brainstorm (≥6).
steps.candidate_md: Ways to make the new site work despite overlap: differentiate format (24/7 vs classes), reprice to expand the pie, target a non-overlapping micro-segment, relocate 0.5mi further, convert the old club's freed capacity to premium PT, cross-sell both sites as one membership, close the older site and consolidate.
steps.interviewer_md:
**Candidate:**
Ways to make the new site work despite overlap: differentiate format (24/7 vs classes), reprice to expand the pie, target a non-overlapping micro-segment, relocate 0.5mi further, convert the old club's freed capacity to premium PT, cross-sell both sites as one membership, close the older site and consolidate.

steps.label: Recommendation (Pyramid).
steps.candidate_md:
